-low salary

Accenture is big on buying up the competition. They bought the company I worked for and then got rid of the raise and promotion structure. I was on track to be promoted at my next review, then Accenture bought us and completely changed how promotions are given. Naturally, everyone was concerned about this. Accenture's explanations about the new structure were intentionally vague. Now, none of us understand what is required to get a promotion.

Accenture has an annual review process to determine raises and promotions. We are not allowed in the process at all. Instead, your "people lead" goes into the meetings to discuss if you deserve a raise or not. I never work with my people lead so how are they supposed to "advocate" for me?

A recurring theme with many who work in this position as contract/vendor Family Ambassadors is that wages are not paid in a timely manner. Many have gone months without being paid, myself included. This is totally unacceptable. When you first take the position, they advise that they aim to pay you twice a month (bi-weekly). However, they make you jump through several hoops to actually receive your due wages.

First, you have to sit down an invoice. Then you must submit it for approval (which could take days or longer). Then you have to wait for it to be approved again. Then you finally recieve an email telling you to "claim your payment". Once you respond, it will take a day or two for you to receive your direct deposit. 

I have never experienced such nonsense with any other employer. 

You can imagine the types of effects it can have on your household finances. When you're expecting your check and it does not come. 

If you value your peace and order, I would not recommend working with them.

pros:
Accenture is an excellent place to kick off your career. The company offers great training programs and plenty of opportunities to build your skills before taking on client work. I felt well-prepared and confident going into projects thanks to the structured learning paths and hands-on support.

The work culture was also a big plus, my seniors were supportive, approachable, and respectful, which made a huge difference when I was just starting out. The benefits package was competitive compared to other companies, and I always felt like I was being treated fairly and professionally.
cons:
While Accenture itself provides a great working environment, your experience can vary depending on the client you're assigned to. Some clients can be demanding and not always mindful of work-life balance, expectations can extend into holidays and late nights.

In one instance, I led a project presentation and shared our UAT metrics. One of the main users was initially confused by the format and requested some adjustments, which we gladly addressed. The user ended up happy and appreciative of the team’s effort. However, another client member who joined late and left early only caught the initial confusion and escalated feedback to my managers, claiming I was unprepared and the users were dissatisfied. Fortunately, the meeting was recorded, and I was able to clarify the situation. It was a stressful experience but taught me the importance of documenting and protecting your work.

title: One of the biggest advantages of flexible work at Accenture is the freedom to choose where and when you work. This autonomy often leads to a better work–life balance, especially when you're able to cut down on commuting time and structure your day around personal commitments. Working remotely or in a hybrid model can also result in cost savings—less money spent on transportation, lunches, or work attire. Additionally, flexibility allows for broader collaboration across time zones and teams, expanding access to diverse perspectives and global talent. Many employees also find that they’re more productive and focused in environments they can tailor to their own preferences.
pros: One of the biggest advantages of flexible work at Accenture is the freedom to choose where and when you work. This autonomy often leads to a better work–life balance, especially when you're able to cut down on commuting time and structure your day around personal commitments. Working remotely or in a hybrid model can also result in cost savings—less money spent on transportation, lunches, or work attire. Additionally, flexibility allows for broader collaboration across time zones and teams, expanding access to diverse perspectives and global talent. Many employees also find that they’re more productive and focused in environments they can tailor to their own preferences.
cons: Despite the clear benefits, flexible work does come with its challenges—most notably, a lack of in-person community. It can be harder to build strong relationships with coworkers when hallway chats, team lunches, and spontaneous brainstorming sessions are few and far between. For new joiners especially, onboarding and feeling truly integrated into a team can take longer without those everyday face-to-face moments. Visibility can also be an issue; in a remote setting, staying top-of-mind for mentorship, sponsorship, or leadership opportunities may require more intentional effort. Lastly, without the natural boundaries of an office, work and personal life can blur, leading some employees to feel “always on” and at risk of burnout.

title: There are absolutely none to give. Accenture treats employees that were outsourced to them badly and will throw them away like trash.
pros: There are absolutely none to give. Accenture treats employees that were outsourced to them badly and will throw them away like trash.
cons: Consultants are treated like garbage by management and leadership. They act like they are the best organization out there but they are quite the opposite. The only thing they know how to do is how to run a contract/project into the ground. Those over the project, including HR, will lie to you all the time, keep you in the dark about everything, not put anything in writing, and not respond to emails because they don't want to put anything in writing. This is the shadiest place to work and no one trusts anyone on the Accenture side. PMs will stab you in the back and turn other consultants on you. Having been a part of an IT department that was outsourced (fired from the previous company and forced to be a consultant to support them) to Accenture, they have no clue on what to do with us and talk down to us every chance they get when there is an actual meeting. Accenture has proven time and time again that they don't have a clue how to run a project in this situation when it relates to outsourcing. We are all on our own and not even the leads know what's going on because Accenture people over the contract won't tell anyone anything, yet, they get upset when people question if we're going to get rolled off or lose our jobs entirely. They lie and tell managers that's not going to happen, yet it happened three months ago and going to happen again in a few months. They also force people into People lead roles, which should be the job of an HR rep, not a consultant. We are basically told we are going to be career counselors and therapists and that we have no say in the matter, even though we didn't sign up for this. They make it known that you have to be billable, yet they force people into roles they didn't sign up for and then use this against them later. HR is worthless. They will not put anything in writing or in an email. If you call them, they won't call you back. This is the worst HR department in my experience. They have no clue what they are doing. Most have avoided them at all costs since they are totally clueless. Those that have worked for Accenture before have also said that nothing has changed one bit. If anything, they have gone down hill. My advice, find a smaller consulting firm if you want this kind of experience. We were forced into this mess and none of us asked for this. Now they are trying to either lay most of us off or roll us off and then force us to find projects that require 100% travel, which none of us want to do as we never wanted to go back into consulting since our former company fired us all and forced us into this disaster. Having been forced to Accenture, they left no room for negotiation and brought us all over at a lower level, with less benefits but they didn't care one bit and HR was very rude throughout the awful transition process. There have been conversations circulating that this contract will end in a few months and that we won't be told until the last minute and when we are told, it will happen before our one year, to avoid paying any of us severance.

title: The only pro as a sales person under N3 LLC that I could possibly find, are my teammates. The people who make up Accenture's Digital Inside Sales departments inside N3 are great people. They're team players, good sales people, and they help teach each other.
pros: The only pro as a sales person under N3 LLC that I could possibly find, are my teammates. The people who make up Accenture's Digital Inside Sales departments inside N3 are great people. They're team players, good sales people, and they help teach each other.
cons:
Everything else. People doing the same role are essentially reaching into a proverbial hat and pulling out random titles, random pay grades, and random tiers. Upper management tries to explain it's based on experience, but people with two years sometimes have a 30% higher base salary than people with six years. The only consistency in better pay is negotiating for it upfront, and hoping you're one of the few of the organization's 800,000 employees who gets picked seemingly at random for a raise each year.

Ok that note, you're also not allowed to laterally change jobs into other roles or departments unless you're on the same project for at least twelve months. You can be working for eleven, the project is cut, and suddenly you're benched and your timer for promotion or a new job is reset and you're stuck again.

Middle management basically liaisons between upper management, entry management, and campaign stakeholders, while entry management does literally nothing but run reports and chastise employees for asking why we're beta testing the clients software while trying to sell new services to the clients' clients. I honestly don't even know what upper management does, but they sure don't seem to pay attention to sales teams or they'd have a fit.

There are so many bureaucratic titles, so much favoritism, and so much management bloat I cannot begin to explain it to you, dearest gentle reader. You can get 3 meetings set up one month, 15 the next month just from blind meeting invites, and then get chastised when the next month you don't get lucky with people accepting blind invites as much. 

There is no sales coach, and no software trainer on the team. Everyone has to learn and teach each other. The managers on site do their best to stop dissent by ending the at least daily meetings when they can't come up with answers to the very pertinent and job-related questions bring asked. They are not teachers and cannot explain to us how anything works, only tell us to submit support tickets to the client for faulty software.

Your KPI and metrics can literally change monthly, I've had four different immediate managers in the last year, and I have yet to meet a single person in their sales structure that is not looking for a new job the entire time they work here.

Further, the favoritism isn't even subtle, it's blatant. There are some managers who literally do nothing but bulldog for others and run reports, who can't even explain basic software principles. There are people on some teams who have zero KPI/metric requirements and essentially run housekeeping or admin for the managers so they don't have to. There are people who have worked for the company for 4-10 years who run obvious circles around others but get passed over for promotions and pay raises because they aren't buddy buddy with the management structure.

There have been multiple lawsuits brought against this company for discrimination, refusal to pay overtime, and favoritism. The company has won only a single lawsuit from the best we as employees have been able to tell, and two new ones are from the last three months alone.

title: Joining the IT consultant industry can be both exhilarating and daunting, and choosing a firm like Accenture can greatly enhance your career trajectory. Accenture offers a unique opportunity for new consultants to network with peers and mentors, fostering connections that are crucial for professional growth. The company’s vast reach and diverse portfolio provide an unmatched gateway into various industries, granting exposure to key stakeholders across multiple sectors. This experience is both inspiring and intimidating, as you navigate the complexities of high-profile client engagements. One of the notable aspects of client work at Accenture is its remote nature. This flexibility allows consultants to collaborate with clients from across the globe, leveraging digital tools and platforms to deliver impactful solutions. The emphasis on remote work not only broadens the scope of projects you can undertake but also encourages the development of skills in virtual communication and collaboration. In summary, starting your career at Accenture as an IT consultant is a remarkable experience that offers extensive learning opportunities, industry exposure, and the chance to engage with top professionals in various fields. It is a journey that promises personal and professional growth, setting a solid foundation for a successful career in the IT consulting industry.

cons:
However, it is essential to acknowledge that the management style at Accenture can sometimes feel condescending and unhelpful. The company's culture tends to place consultants in a continuous state of evaluation, where they are tested and then positioned according to their skills. This approach can be both a blessing and a challenge. On one hand, it pushes you to learn and grow in areas where you may be lacking. On the other hand, it can be overwhelming, especially when management is too preoccupied with their own tasks to provide adequate training.
When guidance is available, it often feels rushed and comes from managers already burdened with their workloads. This dynamic can lead to long hours and working weekends as you strive to acquire the necessary skills to excel. While the learning opportunities are invaluable, the time and energy required to meet expectations can be daunting.
In conclusion, while the journey at Accenture promises remarkable growth and exposure, it is also accompanied by significant challenges. Balancing the rigorous demands of the job with personal growth requires resilience and a proactive attitude. By navigating these hurdles, you will emerge as a well-rounded professional, ready to thrive in the demanding world of IT consulting.

pros: Very smart people.  Lots of resources available.  You work with some of the biggest companies in the world.  Training available covers many industries and technologies.
cons:
Accenture is removing as many jobs from North America as possible and sending them to India and Argentina.  Furthermore, they didn't provide any salary increases for anyone in 2023, and a barely noticeable salary increase in 2024.  With inflation as it is, employees have effectively taken a 10-15% pay cut.  All the while the CEO continues to get massive bonuses.

Typical hard-charging consultant atmosphere.  You are required to find your own next project when you're placed on the bench; there are some resources to help buy not much.  It's like an internal Indeed.com process.  The onus is on you to remain billable, not the company.  You're also required to find your own People Lead to represent you in performance reviews, and finding someone willing to do that is a company of this size is a big challenge.  Agreeing to be a People Lead is a significant time investment and there's little incentive for someone to become a People Lead.
